reasoning:
The euro short-term rate (€STR) is currently at 1.930 (as of February 13, the latest data point before the February 15 baseline). An analysis of the ECB meeting schedule reveals that the next monetary policy meeting is not until March 19, 2026. Therefore, no structural policy rate changes will impact the €STR prior to the two resolution dates (Feb 22 and Mar 17). 

Movements in the €STR will instead be driven entirely by microscopic daily liquidity fluctuations. Over the past couple of weeks, the €STR has shown a tight mode at exactly 1.930 (occurring on 4 of the last 6 business days). Because the resolution criteria require a *strict increase* (>1.930) for a YES resolution, any exact tie (remaining at 1.930) resolves as a NO. 

For the near-term date (February 22), the high probability of an exact tie (a large 'atom' in the probability distribution) depresses the chance of a strict increase well below 50% — roughly 35-40%. For the later date (March 17), the longer forecast horizon increases the spread of the distribution and lowers the probability of an exact tie, pushing the probability of a strict increase closer to 50% (roughly 42-46%), maintaining adherence to the principle that uncertainty should increase and probabilities trend toward 0.5 over a longer timescale.
